data.title: Sanmina: Attractive Growth Path Ahead
data.description:
Investment overview

I give Sanmina (SANM) a buy rating. This is no longer just a diversified EMS name with modest growth. SANM now has much better exposure to cloud infrastructure and a credible path to benefit from the next AI rack buildout through AMD. SANM’s broader manufacturing footprint and better mix also make the business look structurally stronger than before.

Business description

SANM is an electronics manufacturing services [EMS] company that provides integrated manufacturing solutions, components, products, repair, logistics, and after-market services to OEM customers in various sectors, including industrial, defense and aerospace, automotive, cloud infrastructure, etc. SANM has two segments. One is Integrated Manufacturing Solutions [IMS], which includes manufacturing, delivery, and testing of printed circuit board assemblies and high-level assemblies. The second is Components, Products, and Services [CPS], which includes product design and engineering across advanced printed circuit boards, backplanes, cable, fabricated metal parts, precision machined parts, and plastic injection-molded parts.

The ZT deal changes the shape of the company

You cannot talk about SANM without touching on the ZT Systems acquisition. This is not some small bolt-on deal, but one that I view as game-changing. This deal has changed SANM’s revenue mix, its customer exposure, and the kind of programs it can chase after. For context, before the deal, the Communication Networks and Cloud Infrastructure end market made up 38% of revenue, but with ZT, SANM exposure here has risen to 63%.

In other words, this matters because the old SANM was pretty much a diversified EMS business with a lot of slower-moving end-market exposure. This ZT deal effectively moves SANM exposure closer to servers, storage, racks, and AI infrastructure, which, in my view, is the right direction. It pushes SANM closer to the area where demand is significantly strong with structural tailwinds (i.e., demand for AI, which drives the need for more AI data centers). More importantly, ZT also provided SANM with the necessary liquid cooling capabilities, manufacturing facilities in New Jersey and Texas, and relationships with hyperscaler customers, which give a lot of comfort that SANM can start capturing demand from the onset.

Credible path into one of the biggest hardware ramps

Now, besides the ZT deal, SANM itself also has a real path to tap into this AI infrastructure investment cycle. For context, the spending backdrop itself is extremely strong. Hyperscaler data center capex from the top four US cloud providers is expected to grow >60% y/y in 2026, after already increasing 76% in 2024 and 79% in 2025. SANM is well-positioned here through the Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) angle. SANM's agreement with AMD gives it a preferred new product introduction [NPI] position tied to MI400-based Helios racks that are expected in H2 CY26. To be clear, hyperscalers do not need Helios specifically. What they need is rack-scale AI compute that can handle larger training and inference workloads. Essentially, they need to make sure many GPUs can talk to each other at very high speed while power, cooling, and serviceability stay manageable at the rack level. This is why the market is moving from standalone servers toward integrated rack-scale systems. Nvidia (NVDA) is already pushing that model with NVL72-class platforms, and AMD’s Helios is its answer to that.

The size of that opportunity is what makes this exciting. Per JP Morgan estimates, the contract manufacturing TAM tied to AMD racks is worth low-teens billions by 2027, calculated based on the number of GPU shipments, rack systems, and power needed. The important point of this math is that SANM does not need to win all of that to matter. Even if SANM just wins 10% of that contract manufacturing TAM, it is still worth $1.3 billion, which is a big chunk of FY2025 revenue.

SANM footprint moat

My last point on SANM is less exciting, but I think it makes the overall story stronger. The first point here is on SANM’s manufacturing footprint. It has a really widespread (geographically) footprint across the globe. ~49% of manufacturing square footage is in the Americas, ~37% is in APAC, and ~14% is in EMEA. As said above, the ZT acquisition also added facilities in New Jersey and Texas. My view is this scale should be considered a moat since these hyperscalers or clients from defense and industrial systems care a lot about manufacturing location and supply chain resilience. On the former, they want to ensure sensitive systems are built closer to where they are deployed, within jurisdictions they are comfortable with (e.g., avoid tariff situations), and in locations that make support easier. On the latter, they care because supply chain resilience lowers the risk that any form of disruption in one country, supplier, or logistics route delays the whole program. This is especially important when lead times are tight and downtime is costly. Of course, this does not help in driving growth directly, but it should improve SANM’s competitive position when customers decide where to place programs.

Q2 2026 preview

SANM is set to report Q2 later this month. The first thing to watch is whether Communications and Cloud Infrastructure can sustain the strong growth profile it showed in FY25, because that is the clearest sign that the post-ZT mix shift is improving the base business. Second, I want to see that ZT integration is still on track and that management remains confident in the revenue and earnings ramp through FY27. Third, I will also be listening for any comments on AMD-related program timing. My understanding is that the bigger Helios benefit is only expected in 2H CY26, so I do not think Q2 numbers will reflect the full AI upside. What matters more is whether SANM still holds its preferred NPI position. If so, I would think Q2 should support the view that SANM’s earnings power over the next two years is meaningfully better than what the business looked like before ZT.

Valuation

It is not clean to use SANM's 1-year forward revenue growth since they did a major acquisition, so my focus is on the FY2027 growth, which I take as the normalized growth profile. I believe a low-to-mid-teens revenue growth is not a high bar to clear given the strong demand tailwinds that SANM is now well positioned for. The ZT deal gives it the capability to address liquid cooling problems in AI data centers, and the agreement with AMD gives SANM a credible path to ride the next architectural shift in rack systems. If I look across the peer set, SANM's current multiple of ~12.5x two-year forward PE seems cheap. Two peers with a similar growth profile would be Jabil Inc. (JBL) and Celestica Inc.; both of them have high-single-digit growth two-years forward but trade at 19x and 28x two-year forward PE. Even if we say that SANM just trades up to 18x (1x discount vs. JBL as it is smaller in size by revenue), there is still ~40+% upside just based on multiple re-ratings.

Risk

If GPUs for AMD rack manufacturing are not consigned, SANM may need to fund more component procurement itself, which could increase working capital needs, and leverage. One major risk is the succession risk. Jure Sola (the Chairman/CEO) is 75, and it would not be a surprise to see him step down after decades of execution. Any leadership transition could create uncertainty just as SANM is integrating ZT and trying to scale into a much larger AI infrastructure opportunity.

Conclusion

I give SANM a buy rating. I believe the business looks better than it did before the ZT deal. SANM now has stronger exposure to cloud and AI infrastructure, a real path into AMD’s rack ramp, and a manufacturing footprint that should be seen as a moat.

data.title: United Bankshares Looks To Carry Momentum Into Q1
data.description:
This analysis serves as both an update for my previous article about United Bankshares (UBSI) and a preview of what its upcoming Q1 2026 financial report could say about the company’s outlook for FY 2026. When I wrote about UBSI back in June of last year, the company was still working through the integration of a recent acquisition, Piedmont Bancorp. The transaction, which had closed on January 10, 2025, had caused a deterioration of several important performance metrics for United Bankshares. My analysis stated that the company could need several quarters before it sees a noticeable benefit from the addition of its new operations. I rated its stock a Hold at that time.

Fast forward to the end of Q1 2026, and UBSI appears to have turned the corner in a number of positive ways. The stock basically fluctuated in a narrow trading range through the middle of November. Since November 17, UBSI has gained almost 25%, without the inclusion of dividends. The company released an investor presentation on March 2 to highlight the strengths of its operations and outlook for 2026.

In this analysis, I will look back at the strong finish to 2025 from United Bankshares and give a preview of what investors can expect from its Q1 2026 report, which is due to be released at the end of next week. The overall picture for the company has changed as it ended last year more profitable and more efficient than in Q1 and Q2. This improved performance, as well as my expectations that Q1 2026 could hold even more potential for the company, has given me reason to raise my opinion of the stock to a Buy.

Company Snapshot

United Bankshares is the holding company for United Bank, a regional bank that operates in the Southeast and Mid-Atlantic regions. The company also operates United Brokerage Services, which offers its clients financial planning and asset management services. United Bank has over 240 branch locations in eight states plus the District of Columbia. UBSI is dual headquartered in Charleston, WV, and Fairfax, VA, and is currently listed as the 63rd largest bank in the US by asset size, according to the Federal Reserve.

According to the company’s 2025 10-K, United Bankshares controlled $33.7 billion in total assets as of December 31. That was a 12.1% increase from the end of 2024, but about two-thirds of that growth can be attributed to the addition of Piedmont Bancorp. Gross loans and leases from the bank were valued at $24.7 billion, an increase of just over 4% when the acquired loan book from Piedmont is left out of the equation.

Further detail about United Bank’s loan mix can be seen in the table below. Investor commercial real estate makes up the largest loan segment at $8.3 billion, or 33.8% of the total portfolio. Residential mortgages are second in weight at $6.1 billion, or 24.7%. Commercial loans and construction loans are third and fourth in allocation at 15.3% and 14.4%, respectively.

The company’s deposits held at the end of 2025 were slightly lower than the loans on its balance sheet. Customer deposits totaled $27.1 billion, with about 24% of that amount belonging to non-interest-bearing accounts. For Q4 2025, UBSI’s cost of interest-bearing deposits was 2.63%, which is about average for a bank of its size.

United Bankshares is a member of several indices that recognize its 52 consecutive years of increasing dividends, including the Dow Jones Dividend Select Index, S&P High Yield Dividends Aristocrats Index, and the NASDAQ US Dividend Achiever 50 Index. Last year’s increase occurred in Q4 as the company bumped up its quarterly payout from $0.37 to $0.38 per share. UBSI now has a forward yield of 3.49%.

Analysis of United Bankshares

Back on January 22, United Bankshares released its financial results from Q4 2025 and FY 2025. The company was able to end last year with record net income of $464.6 million and record diluted earnings per share of $3.27. That bottom-line performance was 18.9% better than the $2.75 recorded in FY 2024. For FY 2026, earnings growth is expected to be more modest at 8.0%, but the $3.53 EPS prediction may be conservative, as UBSI topped expectations every quarter in 2025. When I last wrote about the company, analysts were expecting just $3.10 in EPS in FY 2025 and $3.20 in FY 2026, which shows just how much the market has raised its overall view of its potential.

There have been several internal improvements from the United Bank that have contributed to its enhanced financial results. Its return on assets ratio finished the last two quarters of 2025 above 1.5%, giving the bank an annual ROA ratio of 1.41%. Return on equity was 9.58% in Q3 2025 and 9.31% in Q4, raising the annual average to 8.63%. This ROE number is well below average for similar-sized banks, but it is a 102-basis point improvement from FY 2024. UBSI’s efficiency ratio has been on a steady decline since FY 2021 and ended last year with an annual mark of 48.5%, 4.17% lower than at the end of 2024.

Net interest margin finished Q4 2025 at 3.83%. This is still below average by six basis points, but it was 34 basis points better than Q4 2024. Data from the FDIC showed that the average bank of the size of UBSI was only able to raise its NIM by 19 basis points over the last 12 months, so United Bank is improving faster than its peers. The biggest driver of this improvement is the bank’s decreasing cost of deposits.

Asset quality appears to be improving for the company as well. The ratio of UBSI’s nonperforming loans dropped from 0.48% to 0.41% in the last three months of 2025. Nonperforming assets decreased from 0.37% to 0.33%. Net charge-offs declined by more than 50% to just 0.15% at the end of 2025.

Expectations for Q1 2026

So what do last year's results tell us about the start of this year? United Bankshares is set to release the results from the first three months of 2026 before the market opens on April 24. Analysts are expecting EPS of $0.85. If the company hits that EPS mark, it will be a 6.6% decline from the $0.91 per share earned in Q4 2025. However, it would be an 11.8% improvement from the adjusted EPS of $0.76 from Q1 2025.

I expect the company to be able to build on the momentum from last year when most of its key performance metrics, like ROA, ROE, and efficiency ratio, were much improved from FY 2024. Wall Street analysts seem to have similar expectations, as UBSI's Q1 2026 EPS estimate has been revised upward on four separate occasions in the last 90 days. The company has a strong presence in some of the East Coast's fastest-growing states - Georgia, the Carolinas, and Virginia. UBSI also stated that it expects to participate in stock buybacks in 2026, which could provide some additional support for its share price and help boost EPS numbers moving forward. As long as asset quality can remain strong, I think Q1 2026 could be just the start of a much improved FY 2026 for United Bankshares.

Risks to Consider Before Investing

Commercial real estate loans make up 42.4% of the gross loans for the bank. Investor CRE comprises 33.8% of the total loan mix by itself. A slowdown in the US economy, especially in the states where UBSI has a footprint, could pressure the quality of those loans and increase the chances for increased defaults. Higher interest rates could depress property values and therefore reduce the collateral available to the bank in the case of non-payment. CRE loans generally offer banks high rates of return, but with greater returns come the risks of greater losses if momentum swings in the other direction.

United Bankshares is heavily dependent on interest income, and therefore the company has increased sensitivity to interest rates. The yield curve has been quite accommodative to rising profits for most regional banks over the last few months as long term rates have ticked higher. Net interest income for UBSI rose $191.1 million, or 21.0%, from $911.1 thousand in FY 2024 to $1.1 billion in FY 2025. Non-interest income increased only $11.5 million, or 9.2%, during this same time. Almost all of those gains in non-interest income were due to a $10.4 million gain on investment securities recorded in Q3. Should conditions change, either with lower long-term or higher short-term rates, the company’s bottom line could be significantly impacted.

Conclusion

When I wrote about United Bankshares last June, I mentioned several “small red flags” that kept me from considering its stock as a buy at that time. Most of those concerns seem to have dissipated now as the company has had more time to integrate its new assets into its operations. With a dividend yield of almost 3.5% and strong operational metrics, UBSI appears to be well-positioned for the challenges that the new year could bring. I am upgrading my assessment of the company to a Buy with expectations of a strong Q1 report next week.

data.title: PML: Munis Bonds At A Discount Make A Lot Of Sense Right Now
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the PIMCO Municipal Income Fund II (PML) as an investment option at its current market price. This fund's objective is to invest "at least 90% of its net assets in municipal bonds whose interest is exempt from regular federal income taxes," and the fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in bonds that at the time of investment are investment-grade quality - bonds that are unrated but of similar quality.

My followers know I cover PIMCO's muni CEFs with some regularity, and PML is no exception. When I last reviewed the fund, I had a positive view of it, but, quite frankly, performance has not lived up to my expectations. While it has posted a positive return, it has lagged even cash over the past six months:

But rather than get discouraged, I view this opportunistically. I see PML as undervalued and think its weak performance of late presents investors with a good time to buy into a tax-exempt income stream. The current discount to NAV, lofty equity valuations, and general strength in the underlying muni sector all point to reasons one could be bullish here. I am therefore keeping a "buy" rating on PML in place and will explain why in detail below.

Why Now? Investors Ignoring The War

To begin, I want to talk about the "why not". After all, equities have been on a strong run, and perhaps now is the time to be getting risk-on and not defensive through munis. If that is your sentiment, have at it, but I'm taking a more cautious approach.

Simply put, the strong run for equities is one of the primary reasons why I think getting defensive here makes sense. The market has begun to discount fallout from the US/Israel conflict with Iran, and I'm not sure that makes sense. Was the previous sell-off perhaps too much? I'd be inclined to agree there. But I'm not sure recent developments truly warrant a complete reversal to the point where equity levels sit above where they were before the war started:

What I take away from this is that my outlook differs from the market right now. I don't think the current conflict will have a disastrous impact on economic growth, but I do feel there should be some war premium baked into stock prices. Until a few days ago there was, but now there is nothing!

The market has recaptured and surpassed prior levels, and the conflict is not resolved or near to resolved, in my opinion. We are one strike away or one tweet away from a flare-up, and now the market is not pricing in any downside impact. I think this is a bit ambitious and want to use my new cash to protect my portfolio for now. This makes munis a reasonable play here.

The Discount To NAV Is Wide

Let's now move into why PML in particular is a reasonable CEF to play this idea at the moment. A primary reason is valuation. My readers know this is always a top concern of mine, and PML passes the test at current prices. Not only is the discount to NAV attractive on the surface, it has widened by over 5% since my last review:

This shows me that PML's underlying holdings are doing just fine. While the total return has been weak since my 2025 article, that isn't because the securities are defaulting or having trouble. It is a result of the market shying away from munis and PML disproportionately. This is why the discount has expanded, despite a slight rise in total return. PML's holdings have been appreciating faster than the market price, and I want to give it on that before the market catches on. This supports my "buy" view going forward.

For Defense, Munis Are a Great Bet

Those who own (or plan to own) PML should recognize this fund is a mix of IG-rated and junk-rated (or non-rated) muni bonds. This presents a healthy, diversified mix in my view - but it is not without risk. Higher-yield securities do have a higher risk of delinquency or default, and readers should understand that going in. PML owns quite a large allocation of securities yielding 5% and above, which tells me a good portion of this portfolio is not IG-rated. (PIMCO does not list out the rating of its securities on the holdings sheet, but it does list the yield, which is how I arrive at the approximate rating of each).

The good news is that I am bullish on both ratings classes right now, and for good reason. As I mentioned, I think munis are a good defensive play, but what sets them apart from other defensive plays is their steady performance over time. We see this by looking at the Sharpe Ratio. This is a measure that not only looks at the returns of an investment above (or below) the risk-free return but also how much volatility that investment has. This is done by looking at moves away from the standard deviation and gives investors a better understanding of how much fluctuation a stock, sector, or fund has seen to achieve those returns.

This is important because many investors don't have the stomach for wild rides. So even if the net return is positive, perhaps it took too many spins and circles on the way to that return. But an investment with a higher Sharpe Ratio will tell you that it achieved its return with less volatility. This ties back to munis in that both IG-rated and high-yield munis have higher ratios than other income sectors:

Hopefully this conveys why I continue to view munis as a "go-to" sector when I am looking for steady income. It doesn't make these other sectors "bad", but it does show that munis tend to be more steady over time. When I am concerned about geopolitical risk (as I am now), this makes munis more relevant for me than ever. This supports why I continue to view PML as a strong play, but this thesis would extend to other muni funds as well.

Fund Flows Starting To Pick Up

Keeping in tune with the broader muni sector, although an attribute to consider is fund flows. This shows how "popular" this idea is with retail traders, and the fact is that munis have been getting more interest as 2026 has gone on. But this is on the backdrop of a very muted 2025, which tells me there is likely plenty of money on the sidelines that could gravitate towards this arena:

I view this in a positive, yet modest, way. This isn't meant to be contradictory but simply to manage expectations. I am not pumping away at this idea as an opportunity of a lifetime. Munis have begun to pique investors' interest, and while I feel there is opportunity for "alpha", we should have realistic expectations of future returns. Flows have been coming in, which shows some are warming up to this sector, so some of the gain has already occurred.

But at the same time, fund flows are at modest levels and coming off a weak year in 2025. This tells me there is room to run, and getting in now is definitely not too late. So for me I will continue to look to this area for value because I believe it exists at the moment and expect that those who buy now will still be getting in before the herd.

Income Metrics Remain Weak

My last two points will be on the risk of this idea. With respect to munis overall, credit quality is strong, and investors should anticipate reliable income. This extends to PML for the most part, but I do have longer-term concerns about the sustainability of the fund's monthly distribution. This isn't because the underlying securities are missing interest payments, but rather because PML is pumping out a yield that consists of both net investment income and return of capital. This is not likely sustainable forever, and with coverage ratios in the 80% range, one would be wise to monitor this closely:

Those who have tracked this fund over time know that these numbers are not overly concerning because it has been this way for a while. And it could likely stay this way for a while longer without any adjustment to the income stream. But it could also change - especially if the coverage ratio weakens if munis stumble. I don't anticipate it but that doesn't mean it is a risk that should be ignored. Weigh this carefully before buying in.

Stocks Could Keep On Rolling

The last point is just to note that while I believe getting cautious here makes sense, not everyone will agree! Markets have been on a roll, and that could continue for sure. That doesn't mean munis will post losses, but it does suggest there could be an opportunity cost to getting too passive here. This all depends on your own personal outlook, how risk-tolerant you are, and what the market ultimately ends up doing!

I say this as we are in the midst of the Q1 US corporate earnings season, and results have come in quite strong. Of course, there is a widespread between top performers and underperformers, but the Tech sector continues to lead the way with impressive earnings growth, and this is boosting the major US indices.

The conclusion I draw here is this is broadly good news, and one could be correct to amplify their equity exposure here. While I don't take that view personally because of the ongoing military conflict in the Middle East, those events do tend to be fleeting, and one could "miss out" by getting too defensive here. But for me, I am comfortable simply not selling my current equity holdings and putting new cash to work in munis. To each his own from here!

Bottom Line

Muni bonds are starting to see a rebound, and I think further gains are on the way in Q2. PML looks like a smart way to tackle this thesis, as the fund has an attractive tax-equivalent yield, a wide discount to NAV on the open market, and holdings that have lower volatility than other debt sectors (as measured by their respective Sharpe Ratios). As such, a "buy" rating continues to make sense for me, and I suggest my followers give the idea some thought at this time.

data.title: ManpowerGroup: Better-Than-Expected Results And Guidance (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm now bullish on ManpowerGroup Inc. (MAN).

Its better-than-anticipated 1Q2026 performance and 2Q26 outlook warrant a valuation re-rating.

My previous October 18, 2024, write-up mentioned the divergence in MAN's trajectory for the near future and longer term.

Q1 Top And Bottom Line Beat

The firm unveiled its latest financials in an 8-K filing issued on April 16.

MAN's year-on-year turnover expansion went from +7.1% for Oct-Dec '25 to +10.3% in Jan-Mar '26. Its 1Q2026 normalized EPS rose 15.9% YoY, representing a reversal from the preceding quarter's 9.8% drop.

The group's first-quarter sales and normalized net income of $4.51 billion and $0.51/share surpassed analysts' projections by 2.2% and 4.6%, respectively.

In my opinion, its solid showing was attributable to Europe's outperformance, AI-related initiatives, and productivity enhancements.

The company's Spanish operations registered the fastest 1Q revenue growth among all the markets at 13% YoY in constant-currency terms. Its French unit turned around from a -3% contraction in 4Q25 to achieve a flattish topline for 1Q26. The latter is MAN's 2nd biggest division by geography, trailing only the United States.

Its robust European showing aligns with SA contributor "Marc Chandler's" analysis indicating that "the eurozone’s manufacturing PMI has risen each month in Q1."

Separately, MAN's results slides drew attention to "$200 million of incremental revenue" generated by its new "AI-powered sales targeting engine." President Becky Frankiewicz also revealed at the April 16 briefing that it "reduced screening time by 67%" with "AI-led interviews."

It's clear to me that the enterprise has utilized technology to boost growth and efficiency.

Adjusted for foreign exchange effects, MAN's 1Q2026 "Selling, General & Administrative" or "SG&A" expenditures were 4% lower YoY. The recent three-month SG&A-to-sales metric was 15.6%, implying a 60 bps decline. These were credited to "strong cost management" in the 8K.

Second Quarter Guide Was A Positive Surprise

I'm impressed with MAN's Apr-Jun forecasts.

The group is anticipating 2Q2026 topline growth and earnings of +5% and $0.96/share, respectively. The sell-side had predicted a slower revenue increase of +4.1% and a more modest bottom line of $0.93.

I've identified two financial improvement levers that should materialize in the current quarter. That explains my confidence in it meeting the ambitious guidance.

At the analyst briefing, it guided for "the U.S. business to flip to low-single-digit percentage revenue growth in the second quarter." Its "Experis" brand suffered from a challenging YoY comparison during 4Q25. This was due to the high base created by a substantial volume of "healthcare IT projects" in 2024. I think this non-recurring headwind shouldn't impact this year's results.

SA News also reported on April 1 that "the U.S. ISM Manufacturing PMI expanded to 52.7 in March, vs. 52.3 consensus." That bodes well for the outlook of MAN's major market, accounting for about one-seventh of quarterly sales.

Also, my take is that the company's AI-related contribution should continue to go up over time. I touched on its "AI-powered targeting engine" in the preceding section. Having debuted in France, this solution will be progressively rolled out to half of its geographical regions by year-end.

It's seeking out other AI revenue-generating opportunities too. Earlier in mid-March, it disclosed the "launch of EXCELERATE AI; a new enterprise AI agent service." The firm described this as a way to "monetize AI" by "supporting companies to redesign workflows" in its earnings presentation.

Becoming Leaner In The Medium Term

Going forward, I expect the enterprise to have a smaller overhead and a more focused business portfolio.

Its 8-K filing highlighted a new goal to realize $0.2 billion of "permanent cost savings in 2028."

Corporate Reorganization Initiatives

Breakdown Of Expense Reductions

As per the charts above, the firm is currently streamlining its client-facing and administrative support functions with technological upgrades. The targeted cost-cutting is estimated to boost MAN's future margins by 110 bps (0.2/18), considering its trailing revenues of $18 billion.

Also, MAN's results call commentary suggested that it will "thoughtfully review our global portfolio" and consider "opportunities to divest of non-core assets."

I believe that its profitability could be enhanced if it parted ways with some underperforming divisions. My Oct '24 piece noted how the group exited "certain lower-margin markets with a higher risk profile like South Korea." It's likely that management takes similar value-accretive actions soon.

You Should Weigh These Downside Factors

MAN's recruitment-related businesses are economically sensitive. A possible worldwide recession triggered by a prolonged Middle East conflict will hurt its financial results.

The company may fail to execute well on portfolio reshaping and cost optimization measures. That could lead to weaker-than-expected margins and earnings.

My Closing Thoughts

I'm raising this name's rating from "Hold" to "Buy."

MAN's "price-to-earnings growth," or "PEG" is appealing at 0.33 times. That's way below the 1x indicative of fair valuation. I arrived at this figure by considering its 8x forward P/E and the consensus FY26-28 EPS CAGR of +24% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

I see the stock eventually commanding a higher multiple in the low-teens, pegged to its 10-year historical mean of 13x. The catalysts include the achievement of its Q2 guide and the '28 $200 million savings.

data.title: Uber: The Profit Machine Awakens (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I may have underestimated Uber (UBER) and its long-term profit potential. The stock has not been immune to recent AI-driven volatility, yet I expect it to be a big AI winner. The company is already driving strong operating leverage, and I expect further strength as AI rapidly improves. I remain of the view that autonomous vehicles represent an important long-term threat, yet acknowledge that they may help increase the size of the market. I am upgrading UBER to a "Buy" rating.

UBER Stock Price

I last covered UBER in January, where I recommended avoiding the stock based on the valuation and my pessimistic view of the future of autonomous vehicles. The stock has fallen around 6% since.

The company’s continued strong results and lower valuation, as well as my growing anticipation of AI-driven efficiencies, have led to an upgrade.

UBER Stock Key Metrics

UBER is a ridesharing, or more specifically, delivery company. The company has built a sticky platform for ridesharing and food delivery from which it aims to expand its addressable markets.

In the most recent quarter, UBER generated 19% YoY revenue growth to $14.4 billion. It is incredible that the company has been able to sustain such aggressive growth rates even this many years after the pandemic. At this point, it is clear that the gig economy is here to stay.

The company generated 35% YoY growth in adjusted EBITDA as margins expanded 40 bps.

The company was profitable on a GAAP basis for the quarter and full-year, generating an impressive 41.7% and 34.4% GAAP incremental operating margin for the quarter and year, respectively.

Ridesharing remains a key part of the business and showed rapid growth in the quarter, with revenue jumping 18% YoY.

Delivery grew at a faster 29% YoY rate but still has room to the upside in terms of profitability, with adjusted EBITDA margins roughly half of ridesharing.

Management believes that the rise of autonomous vehicles would be a tailwind, not a headwind, largely because it helps expand the ridesharing market overall. Management notes that UBER has seen accelerated growth in cities following the launch of AVs.

UBER notes that their platform adds important value through increasing ride efficiencies. I have some doubts regarding this but acknowledge the potential for a growing market.

UBER ended the quarter with $7.6 billion of cash and $9.2 billion of investments versus $20 billion of debt, representing a solid balance sheet.

On the conference call, management called AVs a “multitrillion-dollar opportunity,” but I again am cautious and think it is still too early to tell. Management noted that the company continues “to be aggressive buyers of our stock,” which I interpret as another way to illustrate the commitment to both margin expansion and shareholder returns. Management noted that they are growing increasingly optimistic with regard to delivery advertising, as they now “are seeing that the opportunity size here is potentially much larger.”

Is UBER Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

As of recent prices, UBER traded at around 2.6x sales. Consensus estimates call for solid growth in the coming years, with a trajectory quite resembling a maturing market leader.

The stock trades at reasonable multiples on an earnings basis as well.

I am still a skeptic regarding the potential long-term threats from autonomous vehicles, as I do not share typical bullish views regarding the inability of AV companies to drive growth to their own platforms. On the other hand, I think that I have underestimated the profit potential of this business. I have previously mentioned the 41.7% incremental margin delivered in the most recent quarter and 34% for the full year. These were delivered even before all the recent narrative regarding AI’s potential to drive job displacements.

UBER looks like a clear candidate to benefit from more AI, as I can see efficiencies driven across product development, customer support, and marketing. I am targeting a 20% net margin over the long term but suspect that this might even prove too conservative. That places the stock at around 13.8x long-term earnings. With the company still tackling large growth opportunities in food delivery and ride sharing, that multiple looks more than reasonable. I can see the stock comfortably delivering 12% to 14% returns annually moving forward between ongoing top-line growth, a rising GAAP earnings yield, and minimal multiple expansion.

Uber Stock Risks

UBER might face headwinds in a recession. While I would expect an increase in drivers in the event of AI-driven disruption, this may not be enough to offset decreased demand for the services. It is possible that competitors increase the price competition, pressuring margins. It is possible that autonomous vehicles really do end up stealing share in both ridesharing and delivery.

UBER Stock Conclusion

UBER has shown that strong networks can bolster profit margins in apparently price-competitive industries. I expect it to benefit from this position of strength, as it may help it to further integrate AI-driven efficiencies across its platform. UBER is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading UBER to a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Job Growth Stagnates As Fed Predicts 'Zero Net Job Creation' Going Into 2026
data.description:
According to the March employment report, released last week by the federal Bureau of Labor Statistics, the jobs economy in the United States continues to stagnate as economic growth slows and the US-Israeli war on Iran pushes up energy prices.

According to the report, the establishment survey showed an increase of 178,000 jobs month to month, but the household survey showed a decrease of 64,000 workers over the same period. March’s household survey marks the third month in a row of negative job growth. Over the past year, total household employment has fallen by 661,000, with an average monthly drop of 55,000.

Employment measured by the establishment survey was less dire but showed very anemic growth. If employment numbers over the past year are any indication, March’s job growth number of 178,000 is likely to be revised downward by tens of thousands. In any case, the current numbers show that, even with March’s current optimistic jobs growth total, employment rose by an average of only 21,000 jobs per month since March of last year.

These numbers likely reflect the data behind the Federal Reserve’s most recent report on economic projections, which suggests that US job growth has essentially fallen to zero overall. In March’s FOMC press conference, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell noted the lack of job growth, as described by Yahoo Finance:

“Effectively, there’s zero net job creation in the private sector,” after accounting for revisions over the past six months, Powell said. “But actually, that looks like that’s about what the economy needs, in terms of dealing with very, very low — nonexistent, really — growth in the labor force, which of course we’ve never had in our history.”

Notably, this lack of job growth, combined with a declining labor force, helps explain why the unemployment rate has generally remained flat in recent months. In spite of so little job creation, the unemployment rate for March actually fell slightly to 4.3 percent, down from February’s rate of 4.4 percent.

At the same time, the labor force participation rate fell to 61.9 percent, the lowest since November 2021, and this helps push down the pool of workers who would compete for jobs in a declining market.

New hires, meanwhile, show little promise of resurgence, and in February, hires hit the lowest level since April 2014 (excluding the covid period). This number, however, is not mirrored by a large number of layoffs.

Layoffs remain at unremarkable levels. Stagnant job growth combined with low fires suggests we remain in what has been termed the “no hire-no fire” economy, in which jobs are hard to find, but we’re also not witnessing large numbers of workers losing their jobs.

The Fed’s overall projection for 2026 shows continued stagnation going into the year. In an April 2 report from the Fed, Powell’s words at the March FOMC press conference:

Labor force growth has been slowing and could be near-zero starting this year, driven by weak population growth reflecting low net immigration and by declining labor force participation reflecting population aging. Such weak growth in the labor force is unprecedented in the United States’ recent history. In this note we highlight two significant implications of near-zero labor force growth: First, near-zero labor force growth implies that breakeven employment growth (i.e. the pace needed to maintain a steady unemployment rate) would also be near-zero—making negative job growth almost as likely as positive job growth in any given month. Second, it implies that any growth in potential GDP will need to come entirely from productivity growth. These features would represent a significant shift in U.S. labor market dynamics and the composition of economic growth.

Note that this description of the economy suggests a certain underlying view of the job market. The assumption here is that there is no reservoir of available workers in the United States, and any significant increase in total employment will require population growth, presumably from immigration.

Yet, according to the Census Bureau, there are currently 16.4 million non-disabled men, ages 16 to 64, who are not in the labor force. Moreover, as of March, there are 2.7 million men not in the labor force who report wanting a job. This level is well above what was experienced during most of the 2000s, before the Great Recession.

We might also note that the employment-population ratio is at the lowest level reported since November 2021. In other words, it is unclear that the current lack of job growth must be characterized as largely a function of insufficient population growth.

Even if these discouraged and out-of-market workers can be enticed back into employment, it will be difficult for employers to turn workers into productive inputs as prices rise for producers thanks, in part, to rising energy prices caused by the US-Israeli war on Iran.

The most recent price-inflation data shows producer prices hitting a three-year high. Employers will also face a slowing economy overall. The most recent revision to GDP growth downgraded the fourth-quarter 2025 growth to 0.5 percent. Perhaps it’s no wonder that the latest consumer sentiment index reading has fallen to the lowest level ever recorded.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Netflix Q1: Guidance Is Soft, The Business Isn't
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX)(NFLX:CA)(ZNFL:CA) just reported its Q1-2026 results, and the initial headline numbers are good. EPS beat estimates ($1.23 in GAAP EPS vs. the company's $0.76 estimate), and so did revenue ($12.25 billion, beating by $80 million). Netflix is even launching vertical video at the end of the month.

But the stock still fell 9% to around $98. So, what gives? It's down because of lower-than-expected guidance and news that co-founder Reed Hastings will step down as chairman. The EPS beat was also mainly due to a one-time gain, which I'll talk about more later on.

But investors are ignoring the fact that Netflix's monetization is improving (helping margins expand) and that the company continues to innovate. Plus, the valuation is still rich for a streaming company in a highly competitive industry, but it's lower than usual. On top of that, Netflix is the industry leader, and its growth rate is solid. Based on those factors, the stock can perform relatively well from here. I think it's a cautious Buy.

I'll get into the earnings results and then explain why I'm cautiously optimistic.

Netflix's Results In Detail

As I said above, NFLX beat EPS and revenue estimates. Revenue grew by 16% YoY, or 14% on a constant-currency basis.

Meanwhile, the EPS beat mainly came from the $2.8 billion termination fee that NFLX received recently. Basically, Paramount Skydance (PSKY) outbid Netflix to buy Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), and they then paid Netflix $2.8 billion as a result. Not bad.

But management also said that a higher-than-projected operating income helped EPS results. Operating income grew 18%. That's the more important number to focus on.

The Disappointing Guidance And The Part Investors Are Ignoring

What investors didn't like, though, was the guidance. You can see it below. Q2 revenue guidance of $12.57 billion was below the $12.63 billion consensus, and expected EPS of $0.78 is lower than the $0.84 consensus. Full-year revenue guidance was left unchanged at a midpoint of $51.2 billion, which is fine with me, but it came in below the $51.38 billion consensus.

However, what I noticed is that investors ignored the 31.5% projected operating margin for the full year, which would be an improvement over 2025's 29.5% margin ($13.327B in 2025 operating income on $45.183B in revenue).

If Netflix achieves $51.2 billion in revenue with a 31.5% operating margin, then operating income will come out to $16.128 billion, or 21% higher YoY.

Part of that expected increase could be due to NFLX's improving monetization. Specifically, its earnings release says:

Our recent price changes have gone well, reflecting the strong value we provide members and our advertising revenue remains on track to reach $3B in 2026, up 2x year-over-year.

Doubling advertising revenue is no small feat, and that's higher-margin revenue. The company mentioned that the ads plan is booming. It made up over 60% of all Q1 signups in places where ad signups are supported. Not only that, but Netflix now works with over 4,000 advertising clients, up 70% YoY.

Free Cash Flow Growth

Moving on to free cash flow, it reached $5.094 billion in the quarter, well above where it was in Q1 2025, at $2.661 billion. But again, management explicitly said that the increase in FCF was "driven in part by a $2.8B cash receipt from the Warner Bros.-related termination fee." If it wasn't for that, FCF would have been closer to $2.3 billion, falling YoY.

That's also a reason why NFLX expects $12.5 billion in FCF compared to its previous estimate of $11 billion. Strip away the extra cash flow, and the $11B estimate would be a 16.3% increase from last year's $9.46B. That's still a pretty good growth rate, and it's in line with revenue growth.

Vertical Video Launch

Interestingly, Netflix mentioned that it intends to launch vertical video at the end of this month on mobile, which makes sense because plenty of people watch Netflix from their phones. For example, the recent earnings report stated that "almost half of kids' profiles view content on mobile devices and tablets." It's no secret that vertical video is addictive, and it can keep people on the Netflix app longer. So I view this as a positive.

Reed Hastings To Leave Netflix. Now What?

Reed Hastings has informed us that he will not stand for re-election to our Board when his current term expires at the Annual Meeting in June, in order to focus on his philanthropy and other pursuits.

That's what Netflix's press release said. But he was the co-founder, starting the company in 1997 and revolutionizing the entertainment industry. Now what?

While it's not ideal, I don't necessarily think it's a big blow to the company. On January 19, 2023, Hastings stepped down as co-CEO to become the board's executive chairman.

The stock was trading around $32 then (split-adjusted). But that news didn't stop the stock from trending higher that year and up until it hit its all-time high of $134.12 in 2025. Ted Sarandos and Greg Peters have been the co-CEOs of the company since then, and they've proven themselves, which should comfort investors who are worried about Reed Hastings leaving for good.

Nonetheless, it'll add a bit of uncertainty for now, which the market hates. Investors just need to monitor Netflix's results going forward to see if they can manage without Reed Hastings.

The Valuation Is Rich But Not Unreasonable

Analysts currently estimate NFLX's EPS to come in at $3.16 for 2026, a 25.1% growth rate. I expect that number to remain mostly unchanged based on the unchanged guidance (unless it gets revised higher due to the one-time gain from this quarter, but we can ignore that).

Based on NFLX stock's price of $98 in after-hours trading, that's a forward P/E of 31x. Looking further ahead, EPS is expected to hit $3.88 next year and $4.60 the year after, for growth rates of 22.7% and 18.4%, respectively.

Those are truly impressive growth rates for a company of this size. And they make sense, given the growth opportunity that's left.

While Netflix is already big, the earnings report mentioned that the company accounts for an "estimated ~5% of TV view share globally" and that it has "penetrated less than 45% of our Total Addressable Market (TAM) of broadband households" as of the end of last year.

In short, there's still lots of growth potential here, and the estimates show that.

So, where does that leave us? If Netflix can achieve the $4.60 EPS estimate by the time they report Fiscal 2028 earnings, then the forward P/E right now is around 21.3x, which is attractive.

Even if Netflix's P/E ratio compresses to 30x by then (2.75 years away), the share price would be $138, for a 13.25% CAGR in that period. That's a reasonably good return and a reasonable exit multiple.

Here's why.

Its current TTM P/E ratio, including the one-time gains, is 31.6x. If you strip away the ~$0.65/share in extra gains ($2.8B divided by 4.3B shares), the TTM EPS should be roughly $2.45, giving NFLX a TTM P/E ratio of 40x. And its five-year average P/E ratio is 42.95x.

So, what I'm saying is that NFLX's P/E ratio can drop from 40x to 30x in less than three years, being well under its average, and still generate double-digit returns.

For reference, 30x is the lowest P/E the stock has hit in 3 years. The three-year average is 45.8x.

Risk To Consider

No stock is without its risks. The first one is the valuation. Yes, I just said the valuation is reasonable, but it's not a bargain. And my math for a 13.25% CAGR relies on NFLX maintaining a relatively high multiple of 30x (higher than the current sector median of 13.4x) and the company perfectly meeting estimates.

If anything goes wrong, the returns will be much lower. For example, if the P/E ratio drops to 25x by fiscal 2028, the CAGR will drop to just 5.98%, for a price target of $115. Again, that assumes the company meets estimates. So, the margin of safety isn't great. That's why I'm giving it a cautious Buy rating. It's a solid company at a reasonable price, but it's no bargain.

Another thing is execution risk, with Reed Hastings out of the picture. Although it's not something I'm worried about, it is something I'll monitor going forward after he leaves.

The Bottom Line On NFLX Stock

NFLX's soft guidance and Reed Hastings news sent shares to around $98 in after-hours trading, but I think this makes the stock somewhat attractive now. Attractive enough for a cautious Buy rating.

The stock still has lots of growth ahead, with improving margins from ad revenue and a new growth avenue from vertical videos, all while trading at a slight discount to its five-year average P/E ratio.

I wouldn't bet the farm on the stock, but I think it can still achieve double-digit returns from here on an annualized basis, just as it has consistently in the past.

Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Joby Aviation: Full-Speed Ahead
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY), is now full-speed ahead with the eIPP (eVTOL Integration Pilot Program) in the U.S. The eVTOL manufacturer now has the opportunity to prove out the air taxi thesis without waiting on the FAA certification to ramp up operations and plans for 2027. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish on the stock after this dip back to only $9.

Program Boost

A few weeks back, the White House announced an experimental program to help the eVTOL sector develop into a full-fledged business. A monumental 2026 just took a major leap forward.

The eIPP program gives Joby Aviation and the sector the opportunity to end 2026 with early operations to prove out the air taxi concept, allowing the FAA and DOT to accelerate regulatory coordination to streamline approvals. In addition, the company is working towards carrying passengers in Dubai this year.

The government program awarded Joby Aviation with operations in 10 states: Arizona, Florida, Idaho, New Jersey, New York, North Carolina, Oklahoma, Oregon, Texas, and Utah. In addition, the company has the potential to test the Superpilot autonomous flight technology platform as well:

The program still needs agreements for operational details and won't start until 90 days after these OTA contracts are finalized. The big question going forward is how Joby Aviation will have the aircraft to handle FAA certification, Dubai operations, and 5 different eIPP programs covering options in 10 states.

Joby Aviation reaffirmed plans to ramp aircraft production to four per month in 2027, but these aircraft are needed now. The company has current plans to produce a couple of aircraft each month.

On the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO JoeBen Bevirt has the following to say about the aircraft production plans:

After a year full of rigorous full transition flight testing and meaningful progress across every part of our business, we've begun to shift our focus from how and when we'll go to market to how many aircraft we can produce and where to deploy them.

When the company reports Q1 earnings in early May, the big focus will be the scale of operations for eIPP and the launch of service in Dubai. As the market has seen with robotaxis and autonomous trucks, the scale of operations matters, with Aurora Innovation (AUR) trading at the lows as the company launched services last year with limited trucks in operation nearly a year later.

Joby Aviation confirmed the company has the initial TIA testing aircraft built and is making test flights, with the remaining aircraft needed for testing already in production at the end of February.

Buy The Dip

The stock has seen the market cap dip to $9 billion in the market sell-off. If Joby Aviation sold the ~50 aircraft to be built next year for the $5 million list price, the company would generate about $250 million in additional revenues.

The consensus analyst estimates forecast 2026 revenues of $112 million with a jump to $210 million in 2027. Joby Aviation should report somewhere over $105 million from the Blade operations alone, so the market isn't factoring in a lot of revenues from eVTOLs, though a lot of the original aircraft will initially replace some of the airport services in NYC right now.

Joby Aviation has plans to operate air taxi services, having bought the operations of Blade and partnering with Uber Technologies (UBER). Under this scenario, revenues will scale slower and reach much higher numbers in future periods.

For anyone questioning the revenue potential for Joby Aviation, the company announced a deal last year with Kazakhstan valued at $250 million for aircraft and services. Most investors don't even know where this country is located on a map, and the $905 billion economy is smaller than NYC.

The company starts the year with $2.6 billion in pro forma cash after raising another $1.2 billion in early 2026. Joby Aviation forecasts ramping up spending in 2026 with a 1H target of $340 to $370 million.

The stock valuation definitely needs the business to start producing revenues, and the eIPP program along with operations in Dubai should provide the major catalyst here. Once Joby Aviation can lock in producing 50 aircraft next year with another jump in 2028, the market can start assigning higher valuations to the aircraft stock.

The biggest risk is the company failing to obtain FAA certification in a timely manner or a major accident in the eIPP program. The stock valuation doesn't work if Joby Aviation isn't on a major trajectory towards billions in annual revenue, and any major delay related to certification or due to an accident would crush the plans for material revenue growth starting in 2027.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Joby Aviation is attractive again after the stock has fallen 50% from 2025 highs. Investors should use the weakness to load up on the stock before the company announces the start of operations in the eIPP or Dubai.

data.title: Rocket Lab Enters Europe, Building A Full Defense Stack
data.description:
Rocket Lab (RKLB) has completed the long-awaited acquisition of Mynaric. That is a move that is not only relevant in the national security scope but also in the scope of geographical diversification. The stock has risen 11.4% since my last report, matching my price target. At this point, I am not updating my price target or buy rating, but I want to focus on the importance of the acquisition.

Rocket Lab No Longer Shows Explosive Growth

Before I start the discussion on the importance of the acquisition of Mynaric, I believe it is also a good moment to discuss the fact that Rocket Lab stock is not showing explosive growth as of late and why that is a good thing in my view.

In 2025, Rocket Lab stock showed explosive growth, gaining nearly 175%. Obviously, for shareholders, that is great, and we note that there is support for higher stock prices driven by the commercial and defense space markets. However, a lot of the increase in the stock price was driven by investors assuming a smooth path to certification for the Neutron rocket, which will open up new growth markets for Rocket Lab. However, while the Neutron rocket does provide long-term growth opportunities for Rocket Lab investors, they treated Neutron as a low-risk development, and it definitely was not a low-risk development. The Neutron rocket launch has now been pushed back towards the end of 2026, and I believe that has also resulted in investors taking a more prudent approach towards Rocket Lab’s stock. Not because it is a bad stock or company, but because risks need to be weighed carefully.

Mynaric Provides Critical Laser Communication Terminals

While I wouldn’t say that investors forgot that Rocket Lab is not just a launch provider, the euphoria on Neutron definitely did push the broader and, in my view, more bullish picture to the back. The broader picture is that Rocket Lab is working on becoming an end-to-end provider, vertically integrating parts and satellite manufacturing and launch services. Potentially in the future there are also opportunities for the company to operate its own satellite constellation.

Mynaric is not an unfamiliar company to me. In November 2023, I added the company to my coverage, and what I saw is that the company has a promising product, but the valuation was not quite attractive. Mynaric is a laser communication technology provider that was spun off from the German Aerospace Center in 2009. That spin off marked the start of an effort to commercialize free-space laser communications. You can view Mynaric’s laser technology as the fiber optics of space, providing a high-speed communication layer.

With laser communication technology, a satellite mesh can be created with satellites serving as communication nodes. By doing so, a shorter and faster communication path can be established, and having to “contact ground,” which brings ground relay delays, can be avoided. So, it is not the case that because lasers travel faster, the communication is also faster. Lasers travel as fast as radio frequency waves, but the optic mesh is a differentiator. The other advantages are higher bandwidth driven by the beam width, allowing for very narrow and highly directional beams. That allows the energy to be concentrated in a tiny spot and means that the power per bit is lower and data rates are higher. The result is stronger received signals and a higher signal-to-noise ratio, or SNR. Furthermore, optical frequencies do not rely on licensed frequency bands, making them more flexible.

Mynaric was not the first company to tip its toes in space-based optic laser communication technology, but it differentiated itself by providing a terminal that can be scaled in production. That is in sharp contrast with other laser technology developers that created non-scalable solutions. You can compare it to highly customized cars that cannot be scaled in production, but they do fit a certain demand at a certain price point, and then on the other hand, you have a car that is designed with scalability in mind, which keeps costs lower, unlocking higher demand. With its CONDOR terminals, Mynaric attempted the latter for laser communications. However, Mynaric went public in 2017 at the Frankfurt Stock Exchange, raising just €27 million and another $76 million when the company listed on the NASDAQ. Mynaric pivoted towards defense and constellations and gathered a strategic investment from L3Harris Technologies (LHX) and was selected by Northrop Grumman (NOC) to supply laser communication terminals. Mynaric became deeply tied to constellation programs of the Space Development Agency.

While the company was quite well integrated with customers and programs, the challenge was that it was underfunded against its objective of mass-production of space-grade laser terminals.

Acquisition Of Mynaric Fits Rocket Lab’s Strategy

Now that we know what Mynaric provides, it is time to discuss how it fits in Rocket Lab’s strategy. The simple answer is that the company is growing its Space Systems business inorganically, bringing more and more capabilities in-house, and Mynaric brings in the optic link. However, it goes a bit deeper than that.

Rocket Lab has become a prime contractor and has been contracted by the SDA to provide satellites for the data relay layer, also known as the Transport layer and satellites for missile tracking, also known as the Tracking layer. Moving big amounts of data fast is key for both of these layers, and that is where Mynaric’s solution fits in and where it makes sense for Mynaric to be acquired by Rocket Lab. Not only does it bring the capability for Rocket Lab to implement in the SDA Tracking and Transport layers, it also gives the company a stronger cost positioning. Furthermore, it means that Rocket Lab is becoming an even more important supplier in the aerospace and defense industry with the capability that it can do what Mynaric couldn't, and that is scale production.

Mynaric Acquisition Strengthens National Security Positioning of Rocket Lab

So, Mynaric is another element in the vertical integration strategy and national security exposure. The acquisition of Mynaric is not the first one to strengthen the defense stack. In 2025, Rocket Lab acquired Geost, which builds missile warning, tracking, and surveillance payloads, aligning the company for US Government security requirements. With the acquisition of Geost and Mynaric, Rocket Lab is positioned as an end-to-end missile-defense system provider.

Entering The European Market In A Different Way

Mynaric is a European company. With tensions between Europe and the US increasing and Europe looking for sovereign solutions, demand for European space solutions is increasing rapidly. Sovereign solutions, in some sense, are relative. Europe does not want to depend on US intelligence, but we see that European countries still contract with US-based companies. So, there are opportunities for Rocket Lab in Europe, and the company already reached a framework agreement with the UK Ministry of Defense for engineering and test services for hypersonic weapons and completed its first dedicated launch for the European Space Agency in March.

So, it is not the case that with the acquisition of Mynaric, Rocket Lab is entering the European market. They already have customers in Europe. However, it does give the company a manufacturing and R&D presence. The laser communication terminals are built in Huntsville, Alabama, and in Munich, Germany. The latter is the main production and R&D hub. That is what is new to Rocket Lab in terms of exposure.

For Rocket Lab, the acquisition of Mynaric may not only be the start of scaling production of laser communication terminals and doing more business with European space companies but also buying more space-oriented companies in Europe. That would bolster the European presence, and we note that the European market is more fragmented and relatively undervalued against U.S. peers. While it is not a given that Rocket Lab will engage in a buying spree in Europe, also because regulatory hurdles may exist, it would make sense for Rocket Lab to acquire companies and, in the same way it became more integrated in the US industry, become more integrated in the European industry.

As long as Rocket Lab remains disciplined with its capital deployment, I believe a move from buying the critical choke points in the space-defense value chain to getting more European presence could benefit Rocket Lab.

This Acquisition Does A Lot For Rocket Lab

In terms of financials, Mynaric is not major to absorb since the company was in a distressed position. However, in terms of capabilities, this is a big acquisition for Rocket Lab. It is not just another company added to the Space Systems segment, but it provides critical in-house capabilities in the communication layer and is highly relevant to Rocket Lab’s defense and missile defense stack. Furthermore, it positions Rocket Lab as an even stronger supplier and defense prime in the US as well as Europe, with opportunities for Rocket Lab to consider strengthening its European presence with additional acquisitions at a good price.
